What is the order of reactivity of alkyl halides with ammonia?
explanation: In the presence of an incoming nucleophile, $\mathrm{I}^{-}$ will be released at a faster rate because of its large size. Therefore, order of reactivity of alkyl halide with ammonia is $\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{I}>\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{Br}>\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{Cl}$.

content: Which from following is the most commonly used refrigerant Freon-12?
explanation:
The most commonly used refrigerant Freon-12 is:

Option B: Dichlorodifluoromethane

✅ Explanation:

  • Freon-12 has the chemical formula CCl₂F₂, which is dichlorodifluoromethane.

  • It was widely used in refrigerators, air conditioners, and aerosol propellants until its use was restricted due to ozone depletion concerns.

Final Answer:

B) Dichlorodifluoromethane

content: Which among the following statements is true for haloalkyne?

options.content: Halogen atom is bonded to 'sp' hybridised carbon atom.
Halogen atom is bonded to ' $\mathrm{sp}^2$, hybridised carbon atom of aliphatic chain.
Halogen atom is bonded to ' $\mathrm{sp}^3$, hybridised carbon atom next to a carbon-carbon double bond.
Halogen atom is bonded to ' $\mathrm{sp}^2$ ' hybridised carbon atom of aromatic ring.

explanation:
Step 1: Recall definition of haloalkyne

A haloalkyne is a compound in which a halogen atom is directly attached to a carbon of an alkyne group (–C≡C–).

In an alkyne, each carbon involved in the triple bond is sp-hybridized.

So, in a haloalkyne, the halogen is bonded to an sp-hybridized carbon atom.

Step 2: Analyze options

  • Option A: Halogen atom is bonded to sp hybridised carbon atom.

    ✅ This matches the definition of haloalkyne. Correct.

  • Option B: Halogen atom is bonded to sp² hybridized carbon atom of aliphatic chain.

    ❌ That describes vinylic halides (haloalkenes), not haloalkynes.

  • Option C: Halogen atom is bonded to sp³ hybridized carbon atom next to a carbon-carbon double bond.

    ❌ This describes allylic halides, not haloalkynes.

  • Option D: Halogen atom is bonded to sp² hybridized carbon atom of aromatic ring.

    ❌ This describes aryl halides, not haloalkynes.

✅ Correct Answer: Option A

Halogen atom is bonded to sp hybridized carbon atom.

content: Which among the following is NOT Allylic halide?
explanation: In allylic halides, halogen atom is bonded to a $\mathrm{sp}^3$ hybridized carbon atom next to a carbon-carbon double bond.

content: Which of the following has highest reactivity for $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 1$ reactions?
explanation:
Step 1: Recall nature of SN1 mechanism

  • SN1 reaction rate depends on stability of the carbocation formed (rate-determining step is ionization).

  • Carbocation stability order:

    $ 3^\circ > 2^\circ > 1^\circ > \text{methyl} $

Step 2: Analyze each option

  1. n-Butyl iodide:

  • On ionization → primary carbocation (n-butyl cation).

  • Very unstable. Least favorable.

  1. sec-Butyl iodide:

  • On ionization → secondary carbocation (2°).

  • Moderately stable.

  1. Isobutyl iodide (Isobutyl = $\ce{CH2-CH(CH3)2}$):

  • On ionization → carbocation at the primary carbon bonded to iodide.

  • That gives a primary carbocation, but resonance/hyperconjugation from beta-branches can stabilize somewhat. Still essentially a primary cation, less stable than 2°.

  1. tert-Butyl iodide:

  • On ionization → tertiary carbocation (3°).

  • Very stabilized. Best for SN1.

Step 3: Compare and conclude

  • tert-Butyl iodide produces the most stable carbocation (3°).

  • Therefore, tert-Butyl iodide undergoes SN1 reaction fastest.

✅ Answer: Option D – tert-Butyl iodide

content: Which of the following is NOT obtained when a mixture of chloroethane and 1-chloropropane is treated with sodium metal in dry ether?

content: What type of arenes are obtained when arene diazonium chloride is treated with fluoroboric acid and then heated further?

content: Identify the reagent involved in Sandmeyer reaction.
explanation:
The correct answer is:

Option A

$\mathrm{CuCN} / \mathrm{KCN}$

Explanation:

  • Sandmeyer reaction involves the substitution of an aryl diazonium salt group $(\mathrm{ArN_2}^+ X^-)$ by a halide or cyano group using a copper(I) salt as a catalyst.

  • For example:

    $$ \mathrm{C}_6\mathrm{H}_5\mathrm{N}_2^+ \ \mathrm{Cl}^- + \mathrm{CuCN} \longrightarrow \mathrm{C}_6\mathrm{H}_5\mathrm{CN} + \mathrm{N}_2 + \mathrm{CuCl} $$

  • Copper(I) salts like $\mathrm{CuCl}$, $\mathrm{CuBr}$, and $\mathrm{CuCN}$ are specifically used in Sandmeyer reactions.

Hence, the reagent involved in the Sandmeyer reaction is $\mathrm{CuCN} / \mathrm{KCN}$ (Option A).

Which of the following alkenes is most easily formed by dehydrohalogenation of alkyl halides?
explanation:
According to the Saytzeff’s Rule (Zaitsev's Rule), during dehydrohalogenation of alkyl halides, the more substituted alkene is formed more easily and predominates as the major product.

  • The order of alkene stability is:

    $$ \text{Tetra-substituted} > \text{Tri-substituted} > \text{Di-substituted} > \text{Mono-substituted} $$

    Where "substituted" refers to the number of alkyl groups attached to the doubly bonded carbons in the alkene.

Now, let's analyze each option:

  • Option A: $\mathrm{R}_2\mathrm{C} = \mathrm{CH}_2$

    (Di-substituted alkene; 2 alkyl groups, 2 hydrogens attached to the double-bonded carbons)

  • Option B: $\mathrm{RCH} = \mathrm{CHR}$

    (Di-substituted alkene; 2 alkyl groups)

  • Option C: $\mathrm{R}_2\mathrm{C} = \mathrm{CHR}$

    (Tri-substituted alkene; 3 alkyl groups)

  • Option D: $\mathrm{R}_2\mathrm{C} = \mathrm{CR}_2$

    (Tetra-substituted alkene; 4 alkyl groups)

Most easily formed alkene will be the most substituted one (tetra-substituted).

Correct Answer: Option D

$\boxed{\mathrm{R}_2\mathrm{C} = \mathrm{CR}_2}$

Which alkyl halide has highest bond enthalpy of $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{X}$ bond?
explanation: As the size of halogen increases from F to I , $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{X}$ bond length increases and $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{X}$ bond strength decreases. With decrease in bond strength, it becomes easier to break the bond by applying minimum energy. $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{F}$ bond length is the shortest, so $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{F}$ bond strength is highest and $\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{~F}$ has the highest bond enthalpy.

content: Which among the following has lowest boiling point?
explanation:
The boiling point of a compound generally increases with an increase in molecular mass due to stronger London dispersion forces. Let's analyze the given options:

Chloromethane (CH₃Cl): With chlorine as the halogen, this compound has the lowest molecular mass among the options.

Bromomethane (CH₃Br): This compound has a higher molecular mass than chloromethane due to the presence of bromine, which is heavier than chlorine.

Dibromomethane (CH₂Br₂): This compound contains two bromine atoms, further increasing the molecular mass compared to bromomethane.

Tribromomethane (CHBr₃): With three bromine atoms, this compound has the highest molecular mass among the options.

Considering the trend in boiling points, Chloromethane (Option A), with the lowest molecular mass among the given compounds, will have the lowest boiling point.

content: Which from following compounds is used to prepare a refrigerant R-22?
explanation:
Correct Answer: Option C (Trichloromethane)

Refrigerant R-22, also known as chlorodifluoromethane (CHClF₂), is commonly produced starting from trichloromethane (CHCl₃), also known as chloroform. In the presence of hydrogen fluoride (HF) and appropriate catalysts, chloroform undergoes partial fluorination to yield R-22.

content: Which among the following is not haloalkyne?
explanation:
A haloalkyne is an organic compound containing a carbon-carbon triple bond (alkyne group) with at least one halogen atom attached to the carbon chain. The structure of a haloalkyne typically includes the alkyne functionality, generally represented as $\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{C}$, and a halogen substituent denoted by $\mathrm{X}$.

Analyzing the given options:

Option A: $\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{X}$ does not contain a carbon-carbon triple bond. Instead, it has a double bond, making it a haloalkene.

Option B: $\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{X}$ contains a carbon-carbon triple bond, hence it qualifies as a haloalkyne.

Option C: $\mathrm{CH} \equiv \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{X}$ also contains a carbon-carbon triple bond, indicating it is a haloalkyne.

Option D: $\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{X}$ has a carbon-carbon triple bond, making it a haloalkyne.

The compound that is not a haloalkyne is Option A.

content: Identify the major product formed in the bromination of 2-Methylpropane.
explanation: This is because the intermediate formed is $3^{\circ}$ alkyl radical which is more stable than $1^{\circ}$ alkyl radical.

content: Identify structural formula of DDT.
What is the order of ease of dehydrohalogenation of alkyl halides?
explanation:
According to Saytzeff elimination, the ease of dehydrohalogenation depends on the stability of the alkyl substituted alkenes which is given as,

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{R}_2 \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CR}_2>\mathrm{R}_2 \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CHR}>\mathrm{R}_2 \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CH}_2, \mathrm{RCH}=\mathrm{CHR} \\ & >\mathrm{RCH}=\mathrm{CH}_2 \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad$ The ease of $\alpha, \beta$-dehydrohalogenation is:

$$3^{\circ}>2^{\circ}>1^{\circ}$$

$$\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{Cl}+\mathrm{AgF} \longrightarrow \mathrm{R}-\mathrm{F}+\mathrm{AgCl} \downarrow$$

content: Which among the following is NOT allylic halide?
explanation:
Step-by-Step Reasoning:

Definition of an allylic halide:

An allylic halide is a compound in which a halogen atom (X) is attached to a carbon adjacent to a carbon-carbon double bond (C=C). In other words, the halogen-bearing carbon must be one carbon away from the double bond.

Analyze each option:

Option A: $\mathrm{CH}_2=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{X}$

Double bond between the first and second carbons.

The halogen (X) is attached to the third carbon.

The third carbon is directly next to the second carbon, which is part of the double bond.

Thus, the halogen is on an allylic position.

This is an allylic halide.

Option B: $\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{X}$

Double bond between the second and third carbons.

The halogen is on the fourth carbon.

The fourth carbon is adjacent to the third carbon, which is involved in the double bond.

This is also an allylic halide.

Option C: $\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{X}$

Double bond between the third and fourth carbons.

The halogen is attached to the fifth carbon.

The fifth carbon is directly adjacent to the fourth carbon (part of the double bond).

This is an allylic halide as well.

Option D: $\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{X}$

Double bond between the second and third carbons.

The halogen is on the sixth carbon (if we count from the left: C1 = CH3, C2 and C3 for double bond, C4 = CH2, C5 = CH2, C6 = CH2–X).

Notice that the halogen is NOT directly next to the double bond. Instead, there's an extra CH2 group in between.

This halogen is two carbons away from the double bond, not just one.

This is NOT an allylic halide.

Conclusion: The compound in which the halogen is not on a carbon adjacent to a double bond is the one given in Option D.

Final Answer: Option D is NOT an allylic halide.

content: The correct order of reactivity for reactions involving cleavage of $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Cl}$ bond in following compounds is

content: Which of the following is correct decreasing order of boiling point of compounds?

The molecular size depends upon the size of halogen and number of halogen atoms. Thus, for the given compounds, boiling point decreases as, $\mathrm{CHBr}_3>\mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{Br}_2>\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{Br}>\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{Cl}$.

Thus, n mole $\mathrm{N}, \mathrm{N}, \mathrm{N}$-triethylpropylammonium iodide will give n mole of ethane.

$$\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{N}\left(\mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{CH}_3\right)_3 \mathrm{I}^-+\mathrm{AgOH}$$

$\mathrm{N}, \mathrm{N}, \mathrm{N}$-Triethylpropylammonium iodide

content: Which from following alkyl halides has highest boiling point?
explanation:
To determine which alkyl halide has the highest boiling point among the given options, consider the factors affecting the boiling point, such as molecular weight and the strength of intermolecular forces.

In general, as the size and molecular weight of an alkyl halide increase, the boiling point also increases. This is largely due to the increased strength of Van der Waals forces (dispersion forces) in larger molecules. Among halogens, iodine is the heaviest, followed by bromine, chlorine, and then fluorine.

Let's examine each of the compounds:

Option A: $\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{~F}$ (methyl fluoride)

Option B: $\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{Cl}$ (methyl chloride)

Option C: $\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{Br}$ (methyl bromide)

Option D: $\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{I}$ (methyl iodide)

Given the trend that increasing molecular weight and greater intermolecular forces lead to higher boiling points, methyl iodide ($\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{I}$) would have the highest boiling point among these options, as iodine is the heaviest halogen and contributes to more significant dispersion forces.

Therefore, the alkyl halide with the highest boiling point is:

Option D: $\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{I}$ (methyl iodide).

content: Identify the substrate 'S' in the following reaction.
Which of the following compounds has difficulty in breaking the $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Cl}$ bond?
explanation:
Nucleophilic aromatic substitution involves the replacement of a leaving group (like Cl) on an aromatic ring by a nucleophile.

The reactivity depends on:

Presence of electron-withdrawing groups (EWGs): Such as nitro groups ($-\mathrm{NO}_2$), which stabilize the negative charge in the intermediate.

Position of EWGs relative to the leaving group:

Ortho (o-) and Para (p-) positions: Enhance reactivity due to resonance stabilization.

Meta (m-) position: Less effective in stabilizing the intermediate.

Analyzing Each Compound:

Option A: o-Nitrochlorobenzene

Structure: Chlorine at position 1, nitro group at position 2 (adjacent to Cl).

Effect:

The nitro group is at the ortho position relative to Cl.

Strong activating effect for SNAr due to resonance stabilization.

Conclusion: The C–Cl bond is easily broken.

Option B: m-Nitrochlorobenzene

Structure: Chlorine at position 1, nitro group at position 3.

Effect:

The nitro group is at the meta position relative to Cl.

Less effective in stabilizing the negative charge on the ring during the transition state.

Conclusion: The C–Cl bond is difficult to break compared to ortho and para isomers.

Option C: p-Nitrochlorobenzene

Structure: Chlorine at position 1, nitro group at position 4 (opposite Cl).

Effect:

The nitro group is at the para position relative to Cl.

Provides resonance stabilization to the intermediate.

Conclusion: The C–Cl bond is easily broken.

Option D: 2,4,6-Trinitrochlorobenzene

Structure: Chlorine at position 1, nitro groups at positions 2, 4, and 6.

Effect:

Multiple nitro groups at ortho and para positions relative to Cl.

Highly activates the ring towards SNAr reactions.

Conclusion: The C–Cl bond is very easily broken.

Final Answer:

m-Nitrochlorobenzene (Option B) has the least activation for nucleophilic aromatic substitution due to the nitro group being at the meta position, which does not effectively stabilize the transition state.

Therefore, it has difficulty in breaking the C–Cl bond compared to the other options.

Answer: Option B

content:
Identify the major product obtained in the following reaction.

Chlorobenzene $\xrightarrow[A \text { Anhydrous } \mathrm{FFCl}_3]{\mathrm{Cl}_2}$ major product
Which of the following compounds is obtained by using Swartz reaction?
explanation:
To determine which compounds are obtained using the Swarts reaction, we'll first understand what the Swarts reaction entails and then identify the correct option among the given choices.

What is the Swarts Reaction?

The Swarts reaction is an important method in organic chemistry used for the preparation of alkyl fluorides from other alkyl halides (typically chlorides or bromides). This reaction involves the exchange of halogen atoms (chlorine or bromine) in an alkyl halide with a fluorine atom, using metallic fluorides as fluorinating agents.

Mechanism and Reagents

General Reaction:

$ \text{R-Cl (or R-Br)} + \text{Metal Fluoride (e.g., SbF}_3\text{)} \rightarrow \text{R-F} + \text{Metal Halide (e.g., SbCl}_3\text{)} $

Common Fluorinating Agents:

Antimony(III) fluoride ($\text{SbF}_3$)

Mercury(II) fluoride ($\text{Hg}_2\text{F}_2$)

Silver fluoride ($\text{AgF}$)

Cobalt(III) fluoride ($\text{CoF}_3$)

Detailed Explanation

Starting Materials:

Alkyl Chlorides ($\text{R-Cl}$) or Alkyl Bromides ($\text{R-Br}$)

Reagents Used:

Metallic Fluorides: Compounds like antimony trifluoride ($\text{SbF}_3$) are used because they can donate fluorine atoms to the organic molecule.

Reaction Process:

The metallic fluoride reacts with the alkyl chloride or bromide.

The fluorine atom from the metallic fluoride replaces the chlorine or bromine atom in the alkyl halide.

This results in the formation of an alkyl fluoride ($\text{R-F}$) and a metal halide (e.g., $\text{SbCl}_3$ or $\text{SbBr}_3$).

Example Reaction:

$ \text{CH}_3\text{Cl} + \text{SbF}_3 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{F} + \text{SbCl}_3 $

Applications of the Swarts Reaction

Synthesis of Alkyl Fluorides:

Alkyl fluorides are valuable in pharmaceuticals, agrochemicals, and as refrigerants.

The Swarts reaction provides a convenient method to introduce fluorine atoms into organic molecules.

Industrial Importance:

Used in the production of fluorinated compounds like chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs), though their use has declined due to environmental concerns.

Analysis of Given Options

Now, let's match this understanding with the options provided:

Option A: Alkyl Iodides

Not typically formed via the Swarts reaction.

Iodine is a larger halogen and is usually not involved in such halogen exchange reactions for fluorination.

Option B: Alkyl Bromides

Used as starting materials in the Swarts reaction but not the end product.

Alkyl bromides are converted into alkyl fluorides.

Option C: Alkyl Chlorides

Also used as starting materials in the Swarts reaction.

Alkyl chlorides are transformed into alkyl fluorides.

Option D: Alkyl Fluorides

This is the primary product of the Swarts reaction.

Alkyl fluorides are formed by replacing the halogen (Cl or Br) in the starting material with a fluorine atom.

Conclusion

The Swarts reaction is specifically used to synthesize alkyl fluorides from alkyl chlorides or bromides.

Alkyl fluorides are the compounds obtained through this reaction.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Answer: Option D

content: What is the number of moles of ethane obtained from $$2 n$$ moles of bromomethane using $$2 n$$ moles of sodium atoms in dry ether medium?
explanation:
$$2 \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{Br}+2 \mathrm{Na} \xrightarrow{\text { Dry ether }}$$ $$\mathrm{\mathop {C{H_3} - C{H_3}}\limits_{Ethane} + 2NaBr}$$

$$\therefore \quad 2 \mathrm{n} \text { moles of } \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{Br} \equiv \mathrm{n} \text { moles of ethane }$$

content: Which of the following is formed when propene is heated with bromine at high temperature?
explanation:
When alkenes are heated with $$\mathrm{Br}_2$$ or $$\mathrm{Cl}_2$$ at high temperature, hydrogen atom of allylic carbon is substituted with halogen atom giving allyl halide.

$$\mathrm{CH_2=CH-CH_3+Br_2}$$ $$\longrightarrow$$ $$\mathrm{\mathop {C{H_2} = CH - C{H_2}Br}\limits_{3 - Bromopropene}}$$ $$\mathrm{+~HBr}$$

content: Which among the following is benzylic halide?
explanation: Benzylic halides are the compounds in which the halogen atom is bonded to an sp$$^3$$-hybridised carbon atom next to an aromatic ring.

content: Which of the following compounds has lowest boiling point?
explanation: Generally, boiling points increase with increasing molecular mass due to greater magnitude of van der Waals' force of attraction. Thus, fluoromethane with least molecular mass will have lowest boiling point.

content: Which among the following is a feature of $$\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 1$$ mechanism?
Which of the following is formed when propene is heated with chlorine at high temperature?
explanation:
When an alkene is heated with $$\mathrm{Cl}_2$$ at high temperature, hydrogen atom of allylic carbon is substituted with halogen atom giving allyl halide.

$$\mathrm{C{H_2} = CH - C{H_3} + C{l_2}\mathrel{\mathop{\kern0pt\longrightarrow} \limits_{}} \mathop {C{H_2} = CH - C{H_2}Cl + HCl}\limits_{3 - Chloropropene}}$$

Alkenes form addition product, vicinal dihalide, with chlorine or bromine usually in inert solvent like $$\mathrm{CCl}_4$$ at room temperature.

content: Which of the following gases is formed during oxidation of trichloromethane?
explanation: Chloroform (trichloromethane) when exposed to air and light forms a poisonous compound phosgene $$\left(\mathrm{COCl}_2\right)$$ so it is stored in dark coloured air tight bottles.

content: Which of the following compounds has difficulty in breaking of $$\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{X}$$ bond during nucleophilic substitution reaction?
explanation: Greater the number of electron withdrawing groups at o/p position, greater is the reactivity towards nucleophilic substitution reaction. Electron withdrawing group at meta position has practically no effect on reactivity. Hence, among the given, m-nitrochlorobenzene has difficulty in breaking of $$\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{X}$$ bond during nucleophilic substitution.

content: Which among the following is haloalkyne?
explanation:
Haloalkynes are compounds that contain a halogen atom (such as fluoride, chlorine, bromine, or iodine) directly bonded to a carbon atom that is part of a carbon-carbon triple bond. In other words, for a compound to be classified as a haloalkyne, it must have a functional group that consists of both a carbon-carbon triple bond (alkyne part) and a halogen atom attached to one of the carbon atoms involved in the triple bond.

Now, let's examine the provided options:

Option A: $$\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{X}$$

This is not a haloalkyne because it has a carbon-carbon double bond rather than a triple bond, so it would be classified as a haloalkene.

Option B: $$\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{X}$$

Although this compound contains a carbon-carbon triple bond, the halogen is not attached to one of the carbons participating in that triple bond. Thus, this compound is not a haloalkyne either.

Option C: $$\mathrm{CH} \equiv \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{X}$$

Similar to Option B, this compound has a carbon-carbon triple bond and a halogen, but the halogen is not directly attached to the carbon-carbon triple bond, so this is also not a haloalkyne.

Option D: $$\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{X}$$

This compound has a carbon-carbon triple bond and a halogen atom directly bonded to one of the carbon atoms of the triple bond, making it a haloalkyne.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Option D: $$\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{X}$$

It is the haloalkyne among the given options because it has the halogen atom (X) directly connected to a carbon that is part of an alkyne group.

content: Which of the following reactions is used for the conversion of alkyl chloride to alkyl iodide?
explanation:
The conversion of alkyl chloride to alkyl iodide is typically done through the Finkelstein reaction, which involves the exchange of halide ions. Therefore, the correct answer is:

Option C: Finkelstein reaction

The Finkelstein reaction is an SN2 nucleophilic substitution reaction where an alkyl halide (usually a chloride or bromide) reacts with a sodium halide salt (usually sodium iodide) in acetone to form an alkyl iodide. The mechanism of the reaction ensures that the iodide ion is a better nucleophile in acetone and displaces the chloride or bromide ion. The reaction can be represented as:

$$ R-Cl + NaI \rightarrow R-I + NaCl $$

Acetone is used as a solvent because it helps in the swift precipitation of the sodium chloride formed as a byproduct, driving the equilibrium towards the product side due to Le Chatelier's principle.

As for the other options:

Option A: The Fittig reaction is used to couple two aryl or vinyl halides in the presence of a metal, typically sodium.

Option B: The Friedel-Crafts reaction is a type of alkylation or acylation reaction where an alkyl or acyl group is introduced into an aromatic ring with the help of a Lewis acid catalyst like aluminium chloride, AlCl₃.

Option D: The Swarts reaction is another halogen exchange reaction primarily used for the conversion of alkyl chlorides or bromides to alkyl fluorides using metal fluoride salts such as AgF, Hg₂F₂, CoF₃, or SbF₃.

content: Which of the following compounds does NOT undergo Williamson's synthesis?
explanation: Aryl halides do not give Williamson's synthesis.

content:
Identify major product $$\mathrm{A}$$ in following reaction.

3-Bromo-2-methylpentane $$\mathrm{\mathrel{\mathop{\kern0pt\longrightarrow} \limits_\Delta ^{alc.KOH}}}$$ A
explanation: When alkyl halide having at least one $$\beta$$-hydrogen is boiled with alcoholic solution of potassium hydroxide, it undergoes elimination of hydrogen atom from $$\beta$$-carbon and halogen atom from $$\alpha$$-carbon resulting in the formation of an alkene. This reaction is known as dehydrohalogenation reaction or $\beta$-elimination. The preferred product is that alkene which has greater number of alkyl groups attached to doubly bonded carbon atoms according to Saytzeff rule.

content: Which following statement is true for vinylic halide?

options.content: Halogen atom is bonded to 'sp' hybridized carbon atom.
Halogen atom is bonded to '$$\mathrm{sp}^2$$', hybridized carbon atom of aromatic ring.
Halogen atom is bonded to '$$\mathrm{sp}^{3 \text {,' }}$$ hybridized carbon atom next to carbon- carbon double bond.
Halogen atom is bonded to '$$\mathrm{sp}^2$$', hybridized carbon atom of aliphatic chain.

explanation:
The correct statement for a vinylic halide is:

Option D

Halogen atom is bonded to '$$\mathrm{sp}^2$$' hybridized carbon atom of aliphatic chain.

Explanation:

Vinylic halides are organic compounds where a halogen atom is directly bonded to an $$\mathrm{sp}^2$$ hybridized carbon atom, which is part of an aliphatic chain. This can be seen in structures like vinyl chloride (chloroethene) where the halogen (chlorine) is bonded to the carbon-carbon double bond.

Thus, Option D accurately describes a vinylic halide.

content: Which among the following is NOT a feature of $$\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 1$$ mechanism?

options.content: Involve only backside attack of nucleophile.
In an optically active substrate the product formed is racemic.
Heterolysis of $$\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{X}$$ bond forms carbocation intermediate.

explanation:
The $$\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 1$$ mechanism, or unimolecular nucleophilic substitution reaction, has several characteristic features. Let’s analyze each option provided to determine which one does NOT describe the $$\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 1$$ mechanism:

Option A: Involve only backside attack of nucleophile.

This statement is NOT true for the $$\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 1$$ mechanism. The $$\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 1$$ mechanism does not involve a specific attack direction (backside or frontside) because the nucleophile attacks the planar carbocation intermediate from either side. This is unlike the $$\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 2$$ mechanism, which involves a backside attack by the nucleophile.

Option B: In an optically active substrate the product formed is racemic.

This statement is true for the $$\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 1$$ mechanism. Since the carbocation intermediate is planar, the nucleophile can attack from either side, leading to a racemic mixture if the starting material is chiral.

Option C: Two step mechanism.

This statement is true for the $$\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 1$$ mechanism. The reaction proceeds via two main steps: the formation of the carbocation intermediate (slow step) and the nucleophilic attack (fast step).

Option D: Heterolysis of $$\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{X}$$ bond forms carbocation intermediate.

This statement is true for the $$\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 1$$ mechanism. The first step involves the heterolytic cleavage of the $$\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{X}$$ bond, resulting in the formation of a carbocation intermediate.

Thus, the correct answer is:

Option A: Involve only backside attack of nucleophile.

content: Which among the following is haloalkyne?

options.content: Halogen atom is bonded to $$\mathrm{sp}^2$$ hybridized carbon atom of aromatic a ring.
Halogen atom is bonded to $$\mathrm{sp}^3$$ hybridized carbon atom next to $$\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{C}$$ double bond in aliphatic chain.
Halogen atom is bonded to sp hybridized carbon atom in aliphatic chain.
Halogen atom is bonded to $$\mathrm{sp}^2$$ hybridized carbon atom in aliphatic chain.

explanation:
The correct answer is Option C. Here's why:

Let's break down the terms and the options:

Haloalkyne: A haloalkyne is an organic compound that contains a halogen atom (like chlorine, bromine, fluorine, or iodine) bonded to a carbon atom in an alkyne. Alkynes are characterized by having a triple bond between two carbon atoms.

Hybridization: Hybridization refers to the mixing of atomic orbitals to form new hybrid orbitals with different shapes and energies. The hybridization of a carbon atom determines the geometry and properties of the molecule.

Now let's analyze the options:

Option A: This describes a halogenated aromatic compound. Aromatic compounds have a cyclic structure with a special type of bonding, and the carbon atoms in the ring are $$\mathrm{sp}^2$$ hybridized. This is not an alkyne.

Option B: This describes a halogenated alkene. Alkenes contain a carbon-carbon double bond, and the carbons involved in the double bond are $$\mathrm{sp}^2$$ hybridized. The carbon next to the double bond is $$\mathrm{sp}^3$$ hybridized. This is not an alkyne.

Option C: This describes a haloalkyne. Alkynes have a carbon-carbon triple bond, and the carbons involved in the triple bond are *sp* hybridized. This is the defining characteristic of a haloalkyne.

Option D: This describes a halogenated alkene. The carbon atom involved in the double bond of an alkene is $$\mathrm{sp}^2$$ hybridized.

In summary: Only Option C correctly describes a haloalkyne, where the halogen is attached to an *sp* hybridized carbon atom in an aliphatic chain.

content: Which of the following is least reactive towards $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 1$ reactions?
explanation: $\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{Br}$ is least reactive towards $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 1$ reaction. The reaction proceed via the formation of carbocation in case of $\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{Br}$ the carbocation formed, which is least stable ( $1^{\circ}$ carbocation).

content: What is the name of reaction involving replacement of diazonium group by chloride using cuprous (I) salt ?
explanation:
Sandmeyer's reaction involving replacement of diazonium group by chloride using cuprous (I) salt as reagent or catalyst.

Basically, this reaction used to synthesise aryl halide.

content: Which of the following pairs of aryl halides cannot be prepared directly by electrophilic substitution?
explanation:
Aryl iodide and aryl fluoride cannot be prepared directly by electrophilic substitution because reaction with iodine are reversible in nature and require the presence of an oxidising agent $$(\mathrm{HNO}_3, \mathrm{HIO}_4)$$ to oxidise the HI formed. Fluoro compounds are not prepared by this method due to high reactivity of fluorine.

Aryl iodide + aryl fluoride $$\longrightarrow$$ no reaction.

content: What is correct order of $$C-X$$ bond strength in $$\mathrm{CH}_3-X$$ ?
explanation:
As the size of halogen atom $$(X)$$ increases, the $$C-X$$ bond length increases and hence, the $$C-X$$ bond strength decreases.

Since, the order of size of halogen atom is, $$\mathrm{F}<\mathrm{Cl}<\mathrm{Br}<\mathrm{I}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Thus, the order of } \mathrm{C}-X \text { bond strength is, }\\ &\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{F}>\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Cl}>\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Br}>\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{I} \end{aligned}$$

The correct order of C$$-$$X bond strength in CH$$_3-$$ X is,

$$\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{F}>\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{Cl}>\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{Br}>\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{I}$$

content: The reaction $$2 \mathrm{R}-\mathrm{Cl}+\mathrm{CoF}_2 \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{R}-\mathrm{F}+\mathrm{CoCl}_2$$ is an example of ............... .
explanation:
Given reaction is an example of Swart's reaction. It is an halogen exchange reaction associated with alkyl halides. In this reaction, a heavy metal fluoride is used.

$$\mathrm{\mathop {2R - Cl}\limits_{Alkyl\,chloride} + Co{F_2}\buildrel {} \over \longrightarrow \mathop {2R - F}\limits_{Alkyl\,fluoride} + CoC{l_2}}$$

When alkyl chloride or alkyl bromide is heated with metallic fluoride $$(\mathrm{CoF}_2)$$ then alkyl fluoride and corresponding metal chloride are produced.

content: Which among the following methods is not suitable for the preparation of alkyl chlorides?

options.content: By heating alcohols with thionyl chloride
Chlorination of alkanes in presence of sunlight

explanation:
Chlorination of alkanes in presence of sunlight is not suitable for the preparation of alkyl chloride because chlorination of alkanes gives a complex mixture of isomeric mono and polyhaloalkanes, which is difficult to separate as pure compounds. Consequently, the yield of any one compound is low.

$$\mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{CH}_3 \xrightarrow{\mathrm{Cl}_2 / \mathrm{UV} \text { light }} \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{Cl} +\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{CHClCH}_3$$

content:
Identify the catalyst $$X$$ used in following reaction.

$$\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{Br}+2[\mathrm{H}] \xrightarrow{X} \mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{CH}_3+\mathrm{HBr}$$
explanation:
$$\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{Br}+2[\mathrm{H}] \xrightarrow[\text { Alcohol }]{\mathrm{Zn}-\mathrm{Cu}(X)} \mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{CH}_3+\mathrm{HBr}$$

$$[X]$$ is $$\mathrm{Zn}$$-Cu couple in alcohol. In the reaction, when ethyl bromide is reacted with $$\mathrm{Zn}-\mathrm{Cu}$$ couple and alcohol, ethyl bromide reduced to give ethane. This is more or less a redox reaction. In it, ethyl bromide is reduced to give an ethyl organo metallic and $$\mathrm{Br}^{-}$$ subsequently, the ethanol protonates the ethyl organo metallic to give ethane.

A carbocation is formed in the slow step of an $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 1$ reaction. The positively charged carbon of the carbocation intermediate is $s p^2$-hybridised and the three bonds connected to an $s p^2$-hybridised are in the same plane. Reaction involved is as follows:

explanation: The correct order of boiling point of alkyl halides is $R I>R B r>R C I>R F$. This is because with increase in size and mass of halogen atom, the magnitude of van der Waals' forces increases.

content: Which reaction is useful in exchange of halogen in alkyl chloride by iodide?
explanation:
The reaction is useful in exchange of halogen in alkyl chloride by iodide is Finkelstein reaction. This reaction is used in preparation of alkyl iodide by the reaction of alkyl chloride, bromide with Nal in dry acetone.

$$\text { e.g. } \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{Br}+\mathrm{Nal} \frac{\text { Acetone }}{\Delta} \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{I}+\mathrm{NaBr}$$

content: Nitroalkanes are obtained in laboratory from primary or secondary alkyl halides by the action of
explanation:
Nitroalkanes are obtained in laboratory from primary or secondary alkyl halides by the action of silver nitrite $\left(\mathrm{AgNO}_2\right)$. This method of preparation is an example of nucleophilic substitution reaction. The reaction is as follow:

$R \underset{\substack{\text { Alkyl } \\ \text { hallide }}}{\mathrm{Br} / R \mathrm{I}}+\mathrm{Ag}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{N}=\mathrm{O} \xrightarrow[\text { Alcohol }]{\Delta}\begin{aligned} &\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{NO}_2+\mathrm{AgBr} / \mathrm{AgI}\\ &\text { Nitroalkane } \end{aligned}$

(R can be 1$^\circ$ or 2$^\circ$)
